Introduction

Cubic equations over C\mathbb{C}C

  • For a polynomial equation, a solution by radicals is a formula for solutions using only addition, subtraction, multiplication, division and radicals ⋅m\sqrt[m]{\cdot}m⋅​ for m∈Nm \in {\mathbb{N}}m∈N.

  • For general cubic equation x3+a2x2+a1x+a0=0x^{3} + a_{2}x^{2} + a_{1}x + a_{0} = 0x3+a2​x2+a1​x+a0​=0:

    • Tschirnhaus transformation is substitution t=x+a23t = x + \frac{a_{2}}{3}t=x+3a2​​, giving t3+pt+q=0,p=−a22+3a13,q=2a23−9a1a2+27a027t^{3} + pt + q = 0,\quad p = \frac{- a_{2}^{2} + 3a_{1}}{3},\quad q = \frac{2a_{2}^{3} - 9a_{1}a_{2} + 27a_{0}}{27}t3+pt+q=0,p=3−a22​+3a1​​,q=272a23​−9a1​a2​+27a0​​ This is a reduced cubic equation.

    • When t=u+vt = u + vt=u+v, t3−(3uv)t−(u3+v3)=0t^{3} - (3uv)t - \left( u^{3} + v^{3} \right) = 0t3−(3uv)t−(u3+v3)=0 which is in the reduced cubic form with p=−3uv,q=−(u3+v3)p = - 3uv,q = - \left( u^{3} + v^{3} \right)p=−3uv,q=−(u3+v3).

    • We have (y−u3)(y−v3)=y2−(u3+v3)y+u3v3=y2+qy−p327=0\left( y - u^{3} \right)\left( y - v^{3} \right) = y^{2} - \left( u^{3} + v^{3} \right)y + u^{3}v^{3} = y^{2} + qy - \frac{p^{3}}{27} = 0(y−u3)(y−v3)=y2−(u3+v3)y+u3v3=y2+qy−27p3​=0 so u3,v3=−q2±q24+p327u^{3},v^{3} = - \frac{q}{2} \pm \sqrt{\frac{q^{2}}{4} + \frac{p^{3}}{27}}u3,v3=−2q​±4q2​+27p3​​.

    • So a solution to t3+pt+q=0t^{3} + pt + q = 0t3+pt+q=0 is t=u+v=arg⁡3+arg⁡3t = u + v = \sqrt[3]{\arg} + \sqrt[3]{\arg}t=u+v=3arg​+3arg​ The other solutions are ωu+ω2v\omega u + \omega^{2}vωu+ω2v and ω2u+ωv\omega^{2}u + \omega vω2u+ωv where ω=e2πi/3\omega = e^{2\pi i/3}ω=e2πi/3 is the 3rd root of unity. This is because uuu and vvv each have three solutions indepedently to u3,v3=−q2±q24+p327u^{3},v^{3} = - \frac{q}{2} \pm \sqrt{\frac{q^{2}}{4} + \frac{p^{3}}{27}}u3,v3=−2q​±4q2​+27p3​​, but also uv=−p3uv = - \frac{p}{3}uv=−3p​.

  • Remark: the above method doesn't work for fields of characteristic 222 or 333 since the formulas involve division by 222 or 333 (which is dividing by zero in these respective fields).

  • For general cubic equation x3+a3x3+a2x2+a1x+a0=0x^{3} + a_{3}x^{3} + a_{2}x^{2} + a_{1}x + a_{0} = 0x3+a3​x3+a2​x2+a1​x+a0​=0:

    • Substitution t=x+a34t = x + \frac{a_{3}}{4}t=x+4a3​​ gives reduced quartic equation t4+pt2+qt+r=0t^{4} + pt^{2} + qt + r = 0t4+pt2+qt+r=0

    • We then manipulate the polynomial so that it is the sum or difference of two squares and use a2+b2=(a+ib)(a−ib)a^{2} + b^{2} = (a + ib)(a - ib)a2+b2=(a+ib)(a−ib) or a2−b2=(a+b)(a−b)a^{2} - b^{2} = (a + b)(a - b)a2−b2=(a+b)(a−b): (t2+w)2+(p−2w)t2+qt+(r−w2)=0\left( t^{2} + w \right)^{2} + (p - 2w)t^{2} + qt + \left( r - w^{2} \right) = 0(t2+w)2+(p−2w)t2+qt+(r−w2)=0

    • (p−2w)t2+qt+(r−w2)=0(p - 2w)t^{2} + qt + \left( r - w^{2} \right) = 0(p−2w)t2+qt+(r−w2)=0 is a square iff its discriminant is zero: q2−4(p−2w)(r−w2)=0⟺w3−12pw2−rw+18(4pr−q2)=0q^{2} - 4(p - 2w)\left( r - w^{2} \right) = 0 \Longleftrightarrow w^{3} - \frac{1}{2}pw^{2} - rw + \frac{1}{8}\left( 4pr - q^{2} \right) = 0q2−4(p−2w)(r−w2)=0⟺w3−21​pw2−rw+81​(4pr−q2)=0

    • This cubic resolvent is solvable by radicals. Taking any of the solutions and substituting for www gives a sum or difference of two squares in ttt. The quadratic factors can then be solved.

Galois theory for quadratic equations

Fields and polynomials

Basic properties of fields

  • Definition: ring RRR is field if every element of R−{0}R - \left\{ 0 \right\}R−{0} has mutliplicative inverse and 1≠0∈R1 \neq 0 \in R1=0∈R.

  • Lemma: every field is integral domain.

  • Definition: field homomorphism is a ring homomorphism φ:K→L\varphi:K \rightarrow Lφ:K→L between fields:

    • φ(a+b)=φ(a)+φ(b)\varphi(a + b) = \varphi(a) + \varphi(b)φ(a+b)=φ(a)+φ(b)

    • φ(ab)=φ(a)φ(b)\varphi(ab) = \varphi(a)\varphi(b)φ(ab)=φ(a)φ(b)

    • φ(1)=1\varphi(1) = 1φ(1)=1

These imply φ(0)=0\varphi(0) = 0φ(0)=0, φ(−a)=−φ(a)\varphi( - a) = - \varphi(a)φ(−a)=−φ(a), φ(a−1)=φ(a)−1\varphi(a^{- 1}) = {\varphi(a)}^{- 1}φ(a−1)=φ(a)−1.

  • Lemma: let φ:K→L\varphi:K \rightarrow Lφ:K→L homomorphism.

    • im⁡(φ)={φ(a):a∈K}\operatorname{im}(\varphi) = \left\{ \varphi(a):a \in K \right\}im(φ)={φ(a):a∈K} is a field.

    • ker⁡(φ)={a∈K:φ(a)=0}={0}\ker(\varphi) = \left\{ a \in K:\varphi(a) = 0 \right\} = \left\{ 0 \right\}ker(φ)={a∈K:φ(a)=0}={0}, i.e. φ\varphiφ is injective.

  • Definition: subfield KKK of field LLL is subring of LLL where KKK is a field. LLL is a field extension of KKK.

  • The above lemma shows the image of φ:K→L\varphi:K \rightarrow Lφ:K→L is a subfield of LLL.

  • Lemma: intersections of subfields are subfields.

  • Prime subfield of LLL: intersection of all subfields of field LLL.

  • Definition: characteristic char⁡(K)\operatorname{char}(K)char(K) of field KKK is char⁡(K)≔min⁡({0}∪{n∈N:χ(n)=0})\operatorname{char}(K) ≔ \min(\left\{ 0 \right\} \cup \left\{ n \in {\mathbb{N}}:\chi(n) = 0 \right\})char(K):=min({0}∪{n∈N:χ(n)=0}) where χ:Z→K\chi:{\mathbb{Z}} \rightarrow Kχ:Z→K, χ(m)=1+⋯+1\chi(m) = 1 + \cdots + 1χ(m)=1+⋯+1 (mmm times).

  • Example: char⁡(Q)=char⁡(R)=char⁡(C)=0\operatorname{char}({\mathbb{Q}}) = \operatorname{char}({\mathbb{R}}) = \operatorname{char}({\mathbb{C}}) = 0char(Q)=char(R)=char(C)=0, char⁡(Fp)=p\operatorname{char}({\mathbb{F}}_{p}) = pchar(Fp​)=p for ppp prime.

  • Lemma: for any field KKK, char⁡(K)\operatorname{char}(K)char(K) is either 000 or a prime.

  • Theorem:

    • char⁡(K)=0\operatorname{char}(K) = 0char(K)=0 iff Q\mathbb{Q}Q is the prime subfield of KKK.

    • char⁡(K)=p>0\operatorname{char}(K) = p > 0char(K)=p>0 iff Fp{\mathbb{F}}_{p}Fp​ is the prime subfield of KKK.

  • Note p ∣ (pi)p~|~\binom{p}{i}p ∣ (ip​) so (a+b)p=ap+bp(a + b)^{p} = a^{p} + b^{p}(a+b)p=ap+bp.

Polynomials over fields

  • Degree of f(x)=a0+a1x+⋯+anxnf(x) = a_{0} + a_{1}x + \cdots + a_{n}x_{n}f(x)=a0​+a1​x+⋯+an​xn​, an≠0a_{n} \neq 0an​=0 is deg⁡(f(x))=n\deg(f(x)) = ndeg(f(x))=n.

  • deg⁡(f(x)g(x))=deg⁡(f(x))+deg⁡(g(x))\deg(f(x)g(x)) = \deg(f(x)) + \deg(g(x))deg(f(x)g(x))=deg(f(x))+deg(g(x)) and deg⁡(f(x)+g(x))=max⁡{deg⁡(f(x)),deg⁡(g(x))}\deg(f(x) + g(x)) = \max\left\{ \deg(f(x)),\deg(g(x)) \right\}deg(f(x)+g(x))=max{deg(f(x)),deg(g(x))} with equality if deg⁡(f(x))≠deg⁡(g(x))\deg(f(x)) \neq \deg(g(x))deg(f(x))=deg(g(x)).

  • Degree of zero polynomial is deg⁡(0)=−∞\deg(0) = - \inftydeg(0)=−∞.

  • Only invertible elements in K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x] are non-zero constants f(x)=a0≠0f(x) = a_{0} \neq 0f(x)=a0​=0.

  • Similarities between Z\mathbb{Z}Z and K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x] for field KKK:

    • K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x] is integral domain.

    • There is a division algorithm for K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x]: for f(x),g(x)∈K[x]f(x),g(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrackf(x),g(x)∈K[x], ∃!q(x),r(x)∈K[x]\exists!q(x),r(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrack∃!q(x),r(x)∈K[x] with deg⁡(r(x))<deg⁡(g(x))\deg(r(x)) < \deg(g(x))deg(r(x))<deg(g(x)) such that f(x)=q(x)g(x)+r(x)f(x) = q(x)g(x) + r(x)f(x)=q(x)g(x)+r(x)

    • Every f(x),g(x)∈K[x]f(x),g(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrackf(x),g(x)∈K[x] have greatest common divisor gcd⁡(f(x),g(x))\gcd(f(x),g(x))gcd(f(x),g(x)) unique up to multiplication by non-zero constants. By Euclidean algorithm for polynomials, ∃a(x),b(x)∈K[x]:a(x)f(x)+b(x)g(x)=gcd⁡(f(x),g(x))\exists a(x),b(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrack:a(x)f(x) + b(x)g(x) = \gcd(f(x),g(x))∃a(x),b(x)∈K[x]:a(x)f(x)+b(x)g(x)=gcd(f(x),g(x))

    • Can construct field from K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x]: field of fractions of K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x] is K(x)= Frac(K[x])={f(x)g(x):f(x),g(x)∈K[x],g(x)≠0}K(x) = \text{ Frac}\left( K\lbrack x\rbrack \right) = \left\{ \frac{f(x)}{g(x)}:f(x),g(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrack,g(x) \neq 0 \right\}K(x)= Frac(K[x])={g(x)f(x)​:f(x),g(x)∈K[x],g(x)=0} (We can construct the field of fractions for any integral domain).

    • K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x] is PID and UFD.

  • Definition: f(x)∈K[x]f(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)∈K[x] irreducible in K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x] if

    • deg⁡(f(x))≥1\deg(f(x)) \geq 1deg(f(x))≥1 and

    • f(x)=g(x)h(x)⟹g(x) or h(x) is constantf(x) = g(x)h(x) \Longrightarrow g(x)\text{ or }h(x)\text{ is constant}f(x)=g(x)h(x)⟹g(x) or h(x) is constant

Tests for irreducibility

  • If f(x)f(x)f(x) has linear factor in K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x], it has root in K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x].

  • Rational root test: if f(x)=a0+⋯+anxn∈Z[x]f(x) = a_{0} + \cdots + a_{n}x^{n} \in {\mathbb{Z}}\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)=a0​+⋯+an​xn∈Z[x] has rational root bc∈Q\frac{b}{c} \in {\mathbb{Q}}cb​∈Q with gcd⁡(b,c)=1\gcd(b,c) = 1gcd(b,c)=1 then b ∣ a0b~|~a_{0}b ∣ a0​ and c ∣ anc~|~a_{n}c ∣ an​. This doesn't show fff is irreducible for deg⁡(f(x))≥4\deg(f(x)) \geq 4deg(f(x))≥4.

  • Gauss's lemma: let f(x)∈Z[x]f(x) \in {\mathbb{Z}}\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)∈Z[x], f(x)=g(x)h(x)f(x) = g(x)h(x)f(x)=g(x)h(x), g(x),h(x)∈Q[x]g(x),h(x) \in {\mathbb{Q}}\lbrack x\rbrackg(x),h(x)∈Q[x]. Then ∃r∈Q:rg(x),r−1h(x)∈Z[x]\exists r \in {\mathbb{Q}}:rg(x),r^{- 1}h(x) \in {\mathbb{Z}}\lbrack x\rbrack∃r∈Q:rg(x),r−1h(x)∈Z[x].

  • Example: let f(x)=x4−3x3+1∈Q[x]f(x) = x^{4} - 3x^{3} + 1 \in {\mathbb{Q}}\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)=x4−3x3+1∈Q[x]. Using the rational root test, f(±1)≠0f( \pm 1) \neq 0f(±1)=0 so no linear factors in Q[x]{\mathbb{Q}}\lbrack x\rbrackQ[x]. Checking quadratic factors, let f(x)=(ax2+bx+c)(rx2+sx+t),a,b,c,r,s,t∈Z by Gauss’s lemma f(x) = \left( ax^{2} + bx + c \right)\left( rx^{2} + sx + t \right),\quad a,b,c,r,s,t \in {\mathbb{Z}}\text{ by Gauss's lemma }f(x)=(ax2+bx+c)(rx2+sx+t),a,b,c,r,s,t∈Z by Gauss’s lemma  So 1=ar⇒a=r=±11 = ar \Rightarrow a = r = \pm 11=ar⇒a=r=±1. 1=ct⇒c=t=±11 = ct \Rightarrow c = t = \pm 11=ct⇒c=t=±1. −3=b+s- 3 = b + s−3=b+s and 0=c(b+s)0 = c(b + s)0=c(b+s): contradiction. So f(x)f(x)f(x) irreducible in Q[x]{\mathbb{Q}}\lbrack x\rbrackQ[x].

  • Example: let f(x)=x4−3x2+1∈Q[x]f(x) = x^{4} - 3x^{2} + 1 \in {\mathbb{Q}}\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)=x4−3x2+1∈Q[x]. The rational root test shows there are no linear factors. Checking quadratic factors, let f(x)=(ax2+bx+c)(rx2+sx+t),a,b,c,r,s,t∈Z by Gauss’s lemma f(x) = \left( ax^{2} + bx + c \right)\left( rx^{2} + sx + t \right),\quad a,b,c,r,s,t \in {\mathbb{Z}}\text{ by Gauss's lemma }f(x)=(ax2+bx+c)(rx2+sx+t),a,b,c,r,s,t∈Z by Gauss’s lemma  As before, a=r=±1a = r = \pm 1a=r=±1, c=t=±1c = t = \pm 1c=t=±1. 0=b+s⇒b=−s0 = b + s \Rightarrow b = - s0=b+s⇒b=−s, −3=at+bs+cr=−b2±2- 3 = at + bs + cr = - b^{2} \pm 2−3=at+bs+cr=−b2±2. b=1b = 1b=1 works. So f(x)=(x2−x−1)(x2+x−1)f(x) = \left( x^{2} - x - 1 \right)\left( x^{2} + x - 1 \right)f(x)=(x2−x−1)(x2+x−1).

  • Proposition: let f(x)=a0+⋯+anxn∈Z[x]f(x) = a_{0} + \cdots + a_{n}x^{n} \in {\mathbb{Z}}\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)=a0​+⋯+an​xn∈Z[x]. If exists prime p∤anp \nmid a_{n}p∤an​ such that f¯(x)\underset{¯}{f}(x)¯f​(x) is irreducible in Fp[x]{\mathbb{F}}_{p}\lbrack x\rbrackFp​[x], then f(x)f(x)f(x) irreducible in Q[x]{\mathbb{Q}}\lbrack x\rbrackQ[x].

  • Example: let f(x)=8x3+14x−9f(x) = 8x^{3} + 14x - 9f(x)=8x3+14x−9. Reducing mod⁡7\operatorname{mod}7mod7, f¯(x)=x3−2∈F7[x]\underset{¯}{f}(x) = x^{3} - 2 \in {\mathbb{F}}_{7}\lbrack x\rbrack¯f​(x)=x3−2∈F7​[x]. No roots exist for this, so f(x)f(x)f(x) irreducible in Q[x]{\mathbb{Q}}\lbrack x\rbrackQ[x]. For polynomials, no ppp is suitable, e.g. f(x)=x4+1f(x) = x^{4} + 1f(x)=x4+1.

  • Gauss's lemma works with any UFD RRR instead of Z\mathbb{Z}Z and field of fractions Frac(R)\text{Frac}(R)Frac(R) instead of Q\mathbb{Q}Q: let FFF field, R=F[t]R = F\lbrack t\rbrackR=F[t], K=F(t)K = F(t)K=F(t), then f(x)∈R[x]f(x) \in R\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)∈R[x] irreducible in K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x] iff f(x)f(x)f(x) has no proper factors in R[x]R\lbrack x\rbrackR[x].

  • Eisenstein's criterion: let f(x)=a0+⋯+anxn∈Z[x]f(x) = a_{0} + \cdots + a_{n}x^{n} \in {\mathbb{Z}}\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)=a0​+⋯+an​xn∈Z[x], prime p∈Zp \in {\mathbb{Z}}p∈Z such that p∣a0,…,p∣an−1p\left| a_{0},\ldots,p \right|a_{n - 1}p∣a0​,…,p∣an−1​, p∤anp \nmid a_{n}p∤an​, p2∤a0p^{2} \nmid a_{0}p2∤a0​. Then f(x)f(x)f(x) irreducible in Q[x]{\mathbb{Q}}\lbrack x\rbrackQ[x].

  • Eisenstein's criterion generalises to UFD RRR instead of Z\mathbb{Z}Z, Frac(R)\text{Frac}(R)Frac(R) instead of Q\mathbb{Q}Q.

  • Example: let f(x)=x3−3x+1f(x) = x^{3} - 3x + 1f(x)=x3−3x+1. Consider f(x−1)=x3−3x2+3f(x - 1) = x^{3} - 3x^{2} + 3f(x−1)=x3−3x2+3. Then by Eisenstein's criterion with p=3p = 3p=3, f(x−1)f(x - 1)f(x−1) irreducible in Q[x]{\mathbb{Q}}\lbrack x\rbrackQ[x] so f(x)f(x)f(x) is as well, since factoring f(x−1)f(x - 1)f(x−1) is equivalent to factoring f(x)f(x)f(x).

  • Example: ppp-th cyclotomic polynomial is f(x)=xp−1x−1=1+⋯+xp−1f(x) = \frac{x^{p} - 1}{x - 1} = 1 + \cdots + x^{p - 1}f(x)=x−1xp−1​=1+⋯+xp−1 Now f(x+1)=(1+x)p−11+x−1=xp−1+pxp−2+⋯+(pp−2)x+pf(x + 1) = \frac{(1 + x)^{p} - 1}{1 + x - 1} = x^{p - 1} + px^{p - 2} + \cdots + \binom{p}{p - 2}x + pf(x+1)=1+x−1(1+x)p−1​=xp−1+pxp−2+⋯+(p−2p​)x+p so can apply Eisenstein with ppp.

Field extensions

Definitions and examples

  • Definition: field extension L/KL/KL/K is field LLL containing subfield KKK. Can specify homomorphism ι:K→L\iota:K \rightarrow Lι:K→L (which is injective)

  • Example:

    • C/R{\mathbb{C}}/{\mathbb{R}}C/R, C/Q{\mathbb{C}}/{\mathbb{Q}}C/Q, R/Q{\mathbb{R}}/{\mathbb{Q}}R/Q.

    • L=Q(2)={a+b2:a,b∈Q}L = {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2}) = \left\{ a + b\sqrt{2}:a,b \in {\mathbb{Q}} \right\}L=Q(2​)={a+b2​:a,b∈Q} is field extension of Q\mathbb{Q}Q. Q(θ){\mathbb{Q}}(\theta)Q(θ) is field extension of Q\mathbb{Q}Q where θ\thetaθ is root of f(x)∈Q[x]f(x) \in Q\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)∈Q[x].

    • L=Q(23)={a+b23+c43:a,b,c∈Q}L = {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt[3]{2}) = \left\{ a + b\sqrt[3]{2} + c\sqrt[3]{4}:a,b,c \in {\mathbb{Q}} \right\}L=Q(32​)={a+b32​+c34​:a,b,c∈Q} is smallest subfield of R\mathbb{R}R containing Q\mathbb{Q}Q and 23\sqrt[3]{2}32​.

    • L=K(t)L = K(t)L=K(t) is field extension of KKK.

  • Definition: let L/KL/KL/K field extension, S⊆LS \subseteq LS⊆L. Then KKK with SSS adjoined, K(S)K(S)K(S), is minimal subfield of LLL containing KKK and SSS. If ∣S∣=1|S| = 1∣S∣=1, L/KL/KL/K is a simple extension.

  • Example: Q(2,7)={a+b2+c7+d14:a,b,c,d,∈Q}{\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{7}) = \{ a + b\sqrt{2} + c\sqrt{7} + d\sqrt{14}:a,b,c,d, \in {\mathbb{Q}}\}Q(2​,7​)={a+b2​+c7​+d14​:a,b,c,d,∈Q} is Q\mathbb{Q}Q with S={2,7}S = \{\sqrt{2},\sqrt{7}\}S={2​,7​}.

  • Example: R/Q{\mathbb{R}}/{\mathbb{Q}}R/Q is not simple extension.

  • Definition: a tower if a chain of field extensions, e.g. K⊂M⊂LK \subset M \subset LK⊂M⊂L.

Algebraic elements and minimal polynomials

  • Definition: let L/KL/KL/K field extension, θ∈L\theta \in Lθ∈L. Then θ\thetaθ is algebraic over KKK if ∃0≠f(x)∈K[x]:f(θ)=0\exists 0 \neq f(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrack:f(\theta) = 0∃0=f(x)∈K[x]:f(θ)=0 Otherwise, θ\thetaθ is transcendental over KKK.

  • Example: for n≥1n \geq 1n≥1, θ=e2πi/n\theta = e^{2\pi i/n}θ=e2πi/n is algebraic over Q\mathbb{Q}Q (root of xn−1x^{n} - 1xn−1).

  • Example: t∈K(t)t \in K(t)t∈K(t) is transcendental over KKK.

  • Lemma: the algebraic elements in K(t)/KK(t)/KK(t)/K are precisely KKK.

  • Lemma: let L/KL/KL/K field extension, θ∈L\theta \in Lθ∈L. Define IK(θ)≔{f(x)∈K[x]:f(θ)=0}I_{K}(\theta) ≔ \left\{ f(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrack:f(\theta) = 0 \right\}IK​(θ):={f(x)∈K[x]:f(θ)=0}. Then IK(θ)I_{K}(\theta)IK​(θ) is ideal in K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x] and

    • If θ\thetaθ transcendental over KKK, IK(θ)={0}I_{K}(\theta) = \left\{ 0 \right\}IK​(θ)={0}

    • If θ\thetaθ algebraic over KKK, then exists unique monic irreducible polynomial m(x)∈K[x]m(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrackm(x)∈K[x] such that IK(θ)=⟨m(x)⟩I_{K}(\theta) = \langle m(x)\rangleIK​(θ)=⟨m(x)⟩.

  • Definition: for θ∈L\theta \in Lθ∈L algebraic over KKK, minimal polynomial of θ\thetaθ over KKK is the unique monic polynomial m(x)∈K[x]m(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrackm(x)∈K[x] such that IK(θ)=⟨m(x)⟩I_{K}(\theta) = \langle m(x)\rangleIK​(θ)=⟨m(x)⟩. The degree of θ\thetaθ over KKK is deg⁡(m(x))\deg(m(x))deg(m(x)).

  • Remark: if f(x)∈K[x]f(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)∈K[x] irreducible over KKK, monic and f(θ)=0f(\theta) = 0f(θ)=0 then f(x)=m(x)f(x) = m(x)f(x)=m(x).

  • Example:

    • Any θ∈K\theta \in Kθ∈K has minimal polynomial x−θx - \thetax−θ over KKK.

    • i∈Ci \in {\mathbb{C}}i∈C has minimal polynomial x2+1x^{2} + 1x2+1 over R\mathbb{R}R.

    • 2\sqrt{2}2​ has minimal polynomial x2−2x^{2} - 2x2−2 over Q\mathbb{Q}Q. 23\sqrt[3]{2}32​ has minimal polynomial x3−2x^{3} - 2x3−2 over Q\mathbb{Q}Q.

Constructing field extensions

  • Lemma: let KKK field, f(x)∈K[x]f(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrackf(x)∈K[x] non-zero. Then f(x) irreducible over K⟺K[x]/⟨f(x)⟩ is a field f(x)\text{ irreducible over }K \Longleftrightarrow K\lbrack x\rbrack/\langle f(x)\rangle\text{ is a field }f(x) irreducible over K⟺K[x]/⟨f(x)⟩ is a field 

  • Theorem: let m(x)∈K[x]m(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrackm(x)∈K[x] irreducible, monic, Km≔K[x]/⟨m(x)⟩K_{m} ≔ K\lbrack x\rbrack/{\langle m(x)\rangle}Km​:=K[x]/⟨m(x)⟩. Then

    • Km/KK_{m}/KKm​/K is field extension.

    • Let θ=π(x)\theta = \pi(x)θ=π(x) where π:K[x]→Km\pi:K\lbrack x\rbrack \rightarrow K_{m}π:K[x]→Km​ is canonical projection, then θ\thetaθ has minimal polynomial m(x)m(x)m(x) and Km=K(θ)K_{m} = K(\theta)Km​=K(θ).

  • Definition: let L1/KL_{1}/KL1​/K, L2/KL_{2}/KL2​/K field extensions, φ:L1→L2\varphi:L_{1} \rightarrow L_{2}φ:L1​→L2​ field homomorphism. φ\varphiφ is KKK-homomorphism if ∀a∈K,φ(a)=a\forall a \in K,\varphi(a) = a∀a∈K,φ(a)=a (φ\varphiφ fixes elements of KKK).

    • If φ\varphiφ is isomorphism then it is KKK-isomorphism.

    • If L1=L2L_{1} = L_{2}L1​=L2​ then φ\varphiφ is KKK-automorphism.

  • Example:

    • Complex conjugation C→C{\mathbb{C}} \rightarrow {\mathbb{C}}C→C is R\mathbb{R}R-automorphism.

    • Let KKK field, char⁡(K)≠2\operatorname{char}(K) \neq 2char(K)=2, 2∉K\sqrt{2} \notin K2​∈/K, so x2−2x^{2} - 2x2−2 is minimal polynomial of 2\sqrt{2}2​ over KKK, then K(2)≅K[x]/⟨x2−2⟩K\left( \sqrt{2} \right) \cong K\lbrack x\rbrack/{\langle x^{2} - 2\rangle}K(2​)≅K[x]/⟨x2−2⟩ is field extension of KKK and a+b2→a−b2a + b\sqrt{2} \rightarrow a - b\sqrt{2}a+b2​→a−b2​ is KKK-automorphism.

  • Proposition: let L/KL/KL/K field extension, τ∈L\tau \in Lτ∈L with m(τ)=0m(\tau) = 0m(τ)=0 and KL(τ)K_{L}(\tau)KL​(τ) be minimal subfield of LLL containing KKK and τ\tauτ. Then exists unique KKK-isomorphism φ:Km→KL(τ)\varphi:K_{m} \rightarrow K_{L}(\tau)φ:Km​→KL​(τ) such that φ(θ)=τ\varphi(\theta) = \tauφ(θ)=τ.

  • Proposition: let θ\thetaθ transcendental over KKK, then exists unique KKK-isomorphism φ:K(t)→K(θ)\varphi:K(t) \rightarrow K(\theta)φ:K(t)→K(θ) such that φ(t)=θ\varphi(t) = \thetaφ(t)=θ: φ(f(g)g(t))=φ(f(θ)g(θ))\varphi(\frac{f(g)}{g(t)}) = \varphi(\frac{f(\theta)}{g(\theta)})φ(g(t)f(g)​)=φ(g(θ)f(θ)​)

Explicit examples of simple extensions

  • Let r∈K×r \in K^{\times}r∈K× non-square in KKK, then x2−rx^{2} - rx2−r irreducible in K[x]K\lbrack x\rbrackK[x]. E.g. for K=Q(t)K = {\mathbb{Q}}(t)K=Q(t), x2−t∈K[x]x^{2} - t \in K\lbrack x\rbrackx2−t∈K[x] irreducible. Then K(t)=Q(t)=≅K[x]/⟨x2−t⟩K(\sqrt{t}) = {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{t}) = \cong K\lbrack x\rbrack/{\langle x^{2} - t\rangle}K(t​)=Q(t​)=≅K[x]/⟨x2−t⟩. Then for s=3s = \sqrt{3}s=3​, we have an extension Q(s)/Q(s2){\mathbb{Q}}(s)/{\mathbb{Q}}(s^{2})Q(s)/Q(s2).

  • Define F9=F3[x]/⟨x2−2⟩≅F3(θ)={a+bθ:a,b∈F3}{\mathbb{F}}_{9} = {\mathbb{F}}_{3}\lbrack x\rbrack/{\langle x^{2} - 2\rangle} \cong {\mathbb{F}}_{3}(\theta) = \left\{ a + b\theta:a,b \in {\mathbb{F}}_{3} \right\}F9​=F3​[x]/⟨x2−2⟩≅F3​(θ)={a+bθ:a,b∈F3​} for θ\thetaθ a root of x2−2x^{2} - 2x2−2.

  • Proposition: let K(θ)/KK(\theta)/KK(θ)/K where θ\thetaθ has minimal polynomial m(x)∈K[x]m(x) \in K\lbrack x\rbrackm(x)∈K[x] of degree nnn. Then K[x]/⟨m(x)⟩≅=K(θ)={c0+c1θ+⋯+cn−1θn−1:ci∈K}K\lbrack x\rbrack/{\langle m(x)\rangle} \cong = K(\theta) = \left\{ c_{0} + c_{1}\theta + \cdots + c_{n - 1}\theta^{n - 1}:c_{i} \in K \right\}K[x]/⟨m(x)⟩≅=K(θ)={c0​+c1​θ+⋯+cn−1​θn−1:ci​∈K} and its elements are written uniquely: K(θ)K(\theta)K(θ) is vector space over KKK of dimension nnn with basis {1,θ,…,θn−1}\left\{ 1,\theta,\ldots,\theta^{n - 1} \right\}{1,θ,…,θn−1}.

  • Example: Q(arg⁡3)={a+barg⁡3+carg⁡3:a,b,c∈Q}≅Q[x]/⟨x3−2⟩{\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt[3]{\arg}) = \left\{ a + b\sqrt[3]{\arg} + c\sqrt[3]{\arg}:a,b,c \in {\mathbb{Q}} \right\} \cong {\mathbb{Q}}\lbrack x\rbrack/{\langle x^{3} - 2\rangle}Q(3arg​)={a+b3arg​+c3arg​:a,b,c∈Q}≅Q[x]/⟨x3−2⟩. Q(ωarg⁡3){\mathbb{Q}}(\omega\sqrt[3]{\arg})Q(ω3arg​) and Q(w2arg⁡3){\mathbb{Q}}(w^{2}\sqrt[3]{\arg})Q(w23arg​) where ω=e2πi/3\omega = e^{2\pi i/3}ω=e2πi/3 are isomorphic to Q(arg⁡3){\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt[3]{\arg})Q(3arg​) as ωarg⁡3\omega\sqrt[3]{\arg}ω3arg​, ωarg⁡3\omega\sqrt[3]{\arg}ω3arg​ have same minimal polynomial.

Degrees of field extensions

  • Definition: degree of field extension L/KL/KL/K is [L:K]≔dim⁡L(F)\lbrack L:K\rbrack ≔ \dim_{L}(F)[L:K]:=dimL​(F) Write [L:K]<∞\lbrack L:K\rbrack < \infty[L:K]<∞ if degree is finite.

  • Example:

    • When θ\thetaθ algebraic over KKK of degree nnn, [K(θ):K]=n\left\lbrack K(\theta):K \right\rbrack = n[K(θ):K]=n.

    • Let θ\thetaθ transcendental over KKK, then [K(θ):K]=∞\left\lbrack K(\theta):K \right\rbrack = \infty[K(θ):K]=∞, so [K(t):K]=∞\left\lbrack K(t):K \right\rbrack = \infty[K(t):K]=∞, [Q(π):Q]\left\lbrack {\mathbb{Q}}(\pi):{\mathbb{Q}} \right\rbrack[Q(π):Q], [R:Q]=∞\lbrack{\mathbb{R}}:{\mathbb{Q}}\rbrack = \infty[R:Q]=∞.

  • Proposition: let [L:K]<∞\lbrack L:K\rbrack < \infty[L:K]<∞, then every element in L/KL/KL/K is algebraic over KKK (in this case, L/KL/KL/K is algebraic extension).

  • Tower theorem: let K⊆M⊆LK \subseteq M \subseteq LK⊆M⊆L tower of field extensions. Then

    • [L:K]<∞⟺[L:M]<∞∧[M:K]<∞\lbrack L:K\rbrack < \infty \Longleftrightarrow \lbrack L:M\rbrack < \infty \land \lbrack M:K\rbrack < \infty[L:K]<∞⟺[L:M]<∞∧[M:K]<∞.

    • [L:K]=[L:M][M:K]\lbrack L:K\rbrack = \lbrack L:M\rbrack\lbrack M:K\rbrack[L:K]=[L:M][M:K].

  • Example:

    • K=Q⊂M=Q(2)⊂L=Q(2,7)K = {\mathbb{Q}} \subset M = {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2}) \subset L = {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{7})K=Q⊂M=Q(2​)⊂L=Q(2​,7​). M/KM/KM/K has basis {1,2}\left\{ 1,\sqrt{2} \right\}{1,2​} so [M:K]=2\lbrack M:K\rbrack = 2[M:K]=2. Let 7∈Q(2)\sqrt{7} \in {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2})7​∈Q(2​), then 7=c+d2\sqrt{7} = c + d\sqrt{2}7​=c+d2​, c,d∈Qc,d \in {\mathbb{Q}}c,d∈Q so 7=(c2+2d2)+2cd27 = \left( c^{2} + 2d^{2} \right) + 2cd\sqrt{2}7=(c2+2d2)+2cd2​ so 7=c2+2d27 = c^{2} + 2d^{2}7=c2+2d2, 0=2cd0 = 2cd0=2cd so d2=72d^{2} = \frac{7}{2}d2=27​ or c2=7c^{2} = 7c2=7, which are both contradictions. So [L:K]=4\lbrack L:K\rbrack = 4[L:K]=4 with basis {1,2,7,14}\{ 1,\sqrt{2},\sqrt{7},\sqrt{14}\}{1,2​,7​,14​}.

    • Let K=Q⊂M=Q(i)⊂Q(i,2)K = {\mathbb{Q}} \subset M = {\mathbb{Q}}(i) \subset {\mathbb{Q}}(i,\sqrt{2})K=Q⊂M=Q(i)⊂Q(i,2​). We know [Q(i):Q]=2\left\lbrack {\mathbb{Q}}(i):{\mathbb{Q}} \right\rbrack = 2[Q(i):Q]=2, and [Q(2):Q]=2\lbrack{\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2}):{\mathbb{Q}}\rbrack = 2[Q(2​):Q]=2, [Q(i,2):Q]=2\lbrack{\mathbb{Q}}(i,\sqrt{2}):{\mathbb{Q}}\rbrack = 2[Q(i,2​):Q]=2 (since i∉Ri \notin {\mathbb{R}}i∈/R) so [Q(i,2):Q(2)]=2\lbrack{\mathbb{Q}}(i,\sqrt{2}):{\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2})\rbrack = 2[Q(i,2​):Q(2​)]=2.

    • Let K=Q⊂M=Q(2)⊂L=Q(2,arg⁡3)K = {\mathbb{Q}} \subset M = {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2}) \subset L = {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt[3]{\arg})K=Q⊂M=Q(2​)⊂L=Q(2​,3arg​). Then [Q(2):Q]=2\lbrack{\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2}):{\mathbb{Q}}\rbrack = 2[Q(2​):Q]=2, [Q(arg⁡3):Q]=3\lbrack{\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt[3]{\arg}):{\mathbb{Q}}\rbrack = 3[Q(3arg​):Q]=3 so 2 ∣ [L:K]2~|~\lbrack L:K\rbrack2 ∣ [L:K] and 3 ∣ [L:K]3~|~\lbrack L:K\rbrack3 ∣ [L:K] so 6 ∣ [L:K]6~|~\lbrack L:K\rbrack6 ∣ [L:K] so [L:K]≥6\lbrack L:K\rbrack \geq 6[L:K]≥6. But [L:M]≤3\lbrack L:M\rbrack \leq 3[L:M]≤3 and [M:K]≤2\lbrack M:K\rbrack \leq 2[M:K]≤2 so [L:K]≤6\lbrack L:K\rbrack \leq 6[L:K]≤6 hence [L:K]=6\lbrack L:K\rbrack = 6[L:K]=6.

  • More generally, we have [K(α,β):K]≤[K(α):K][K(β):K]\left\lbrack K(\alpha,\beta):K \right\rbrack \leq \left\lbrack K(\alpha):K \right\rbrack\left\lbrack K(\beta):K \right\rbrack[K(α,β):K]≤[K(α):K][K(β):K].

  • Example:

    • Let θ=arg⁡3+1\theta = \sqrt[3]{\arg} + 1θ=3arg​+1. Q(θ)=Q(arg⁡3){\mathbb{Q}}(\theta) = {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt[3]{\arg})Q(θ)=Q(3arg​) so minimal polynomial over Q\mathbb{Q}Q, mmm, has deg⁡(m)=3\deg(m) = 3deg(m)=3. (θ−1)3=4(\theta - 1)^{3} = 4(θ−1)3=4 so minimal polynomial is x3−3x2+3x−5x^{3} - 3x^{2} + 3x - 5x3−3x2+3x−5.

    • Let θ=2+3\theta = \sqrt{2} + \sqrt{3}θ=2​+3​. Q(2,θ)=Q(2,3){\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2},\theta) = {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})Q(2​,θ)=Q(2​,3​) which has degree 222 over Q(2){\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2})Q(2​) so minimal polynomial of θ\thetaθ over Q(2){\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2})Q(2​) has degree 222, (θ−2)=3\left( \theta - \sqrt{2} \right) = \sqrt{3}(θ−2​)=3​ so minimal polynomial is x2−22x−1x^{2} - 2\sqrt{2}x - 1x2−22​x−1.

    • Let θ=2+3\theta = \sqrt{2} + \sqrt{3}θ=2​+3​. Q⊂Q(θ)⊂Q(2,7){\mathbb{Q}} \subset {\mathbb{Q}}(\theta) \subset {\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{7})Q⊂Q(θ)⊂Q(2​,7​) so [Q(θ):Q] ∣ [Q(2,3):Q]=4\left\lbrack {\mathbb{Q}}(\theta):{\mathbb{Q}} \right\rbrack~|~\lbrack{\mathbb{Q}}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):{\mathbb{Q}}\rbrack = 4[Q(θ):Q] ∣ [Q(2​,3​):Q]=4 so [Q(θ):Q]∈{1,2,4}\left\lbrack {\mathbb{Q}}(\theta):{\mathbb{Q}} \right\rbrack \in \left\{ 1,2,4 \right\}[Q(θ):Q]∈{1,2,4}. Can't be 111 as θ∉Q\theta \notin {\mathbb{Q}}θ∈/Q. If it was 222 then 1,θ,θ21,\theta,\theta^{2}1,θ,θ2 are linearly dependent over Q\mathbb{Q}Q which leads to a contradiction. So degree of minimal polynomial of θ\thetaθ over Q\mathbb{Q}Q is 444. θ2=5+26⇒(θ2−5)2=24\theta^{2} = 5 + 2\sqrt{6} \Rightarrow \left( \theta^{2} - 5 \right)^{2} = 24θ2=5+26​⇒(θ2−5)2=24 so minimal polynomial is x4−10x2+1x^{4} - 10x^{2} + 1x4−10x2+1.
